paragraphs: We cannot hope to paint the daily life of our Romani people in its reality unless we first attempt to create a balanced understanding of the setting in which it was lived and shaped, and free ourselves from misconceptions about it. We can satisfactorily study the method in which the Romans lived their lives only after we have mapped out the key elements of the framework that defined their time, both within and outside of which the routine of their daily life would become increasingly obscure. The interior was divided into five naves, each 130 metres long. The central nave reached a width of 25 metres. There were 96 pillars in total. The entire floor was paved with Luna marble, and the roof was adorned with tiles of gilded bronze.

By classical times, however, it had lost its significance as a boundary for the city.

If five of the fourteen regions of Augustus were contained within the ancient city's circuit, five others lay partly within and partly without, while the remaining four were completely outside: the fifth (Esquiline), the seventh (Via Lata), the ninth (Circus Flaminius), and the fourteenth (Transtiberina). After the triumph of Julius Caesar, the population made another upward bound. The Annona required a significant quantity of grain each year to support the population. According to Aurelius Victor, Egypt supplied approximately 4,669,000 bushels. Josephus notes that Africa provided twice this amount, resulting in a total of around 9,338,000 bushels. Allowing an average yearly consumption of 60 modii (14.006 bushels) per head, this would represent approximately 1 million recipients. All children under 11 years old were excluded.

To a Latin scholar, the word domus implies a hereditary property. It means a private house that is undivided and where only the owner and their family live. In contrast, the insula, as its name suggests, refers to a large isolated building that serves as an interest-bearing piece of real estate. The insula is subdivided into several flats or cenacula, each housing one or more tenants or families of tenants.

We are therefore not justified in dividing the islands among the households of our statistics. It is safe to assume that the mansion known as the House of Livia on the Palatine, 8, and the House of the Gamala at Ostia, which later came into the possession of Apuleius, shared similarities with the country houses of Herculaneum and Pompeii. Similarly, it can be inferred that the private mansions of wealthy individuals, as noted in the Regionaries, often incorporated similar features. The domus, influenced by Hellenistic architecture, spread horizontally. The insula, likely originating in the 4th century BC, developed in a similar manner. During the empire period, buildings reached an astonishing height, considering the era's standards. In Caesar's time, Caelius paid a yearly rent of 30,000 sesterces. This arrangement allowed for easier visualization, as many of these structures still remain intact today, including those found along the Via Biberatica and at Ostia. Most of the windows featured miniature gardens, formed from pots of flowers, much like those described by the Roman naturalist Pliny the Elder. Even after the brick technique had been perfected in the second century, and it had become usual to cover the entire facade with bricks, the city was constantly filled with the noise of buildings collapsing or being torn down to prevent collapse; and the tenants of an insula lived in constant expectation of its coming down on their heads. This was because, in the first place, they were unsubstantial. Further, the weight of their floors involved the introduction of massive wooden beams, and the movable stoves which heated them posed a perpetual risk of fire. The candles, smoky lamps, and torches that lit them at night also carried this risk. Finally, as we shall see, water was issued to the various storeys with reluctance.

In spite of the attention Trajan paid to the policing of Rome's city center, daily fires were a common occurrence in Roman life. Apart from their statues of marble and bronze, their furniture was sparse. Wealth was displayed not in the quantity of items, but in the quality of the pieces, the precious materials used, and the unique shapes that reflected their owner's taste. For every Roman, the primary piece of furniture was the bed (lectus), where they would sleep during their siestas and at night. By day, they would recline on it to eat, read, write, or receive visitors. Humble people shifted with a shelf of masonry built along the wall and covered with a pallet.

Therefore, it is unlikely that the windows in these places were equipped with fine, transparent sheets of lapis specularis, which were sometimes used by wealthy families to screen alcoves in bedrooms, bathrooms, or garden hothouses, or even sedan chairs. They could not have been installed with the thick, opaque glass panes that remain in place in the skylight windows of the ancient Roman baths at Herculaneum and Pompeii. These panes provided a hermetic seal to retain heat while preventing complete darkness. The inhabitants of a Roman house must have taken inadequate measures to protect themselves. They might have used hanging cloths or animal skins that were blown by the wind or drenched by rain. Alternatively, they could have overprotected themselves with shutters made of one or two leaves, which would keep out cold and rain but also block sunlight during midsummer and winter winds.

The heating arrangements in the insula were extremely defective. As the atrium had been dispensed with, and the cenacula were piled one above the other, it was impossible for the inhabitants of an insula to enjoy the luxury common to the peasantry. Gathering round the fire lighted by the womenfolk in the centre of their hovels, while sparks and smoke escaped through the gaping hole purposely left in the roof. At the pinnacle of the social hierarchy stood the Senatorial Order. A member of this order had to own at least $40,000. Among the knights, a distinguished man (vir egregius) served as a procurator, while a very perfect man (vir perfectissimus) held the position of prefect. However, if he were a praetor, he would be considered most eminent (vir eminentissimus), a title later revived by the Roman Church for its cardinals. The epithet "most famous" (vir clarissimus) was reserved for senators and their immediate relatives. This exact and rigid system, which was devised by Peter the Great, anticipates an elaborate hierarchy. Its variations are paralleled by Napoleon's system of graded precedence in the army and the Legion of Honour.

In another sense, however, this primacy implied a fundamental distinction between himself and the rest of humanity. As the emperor, as incarnation of the law and guardian of auspices, he was closer to the gods than to ordinary human beings. His sacred character as Augustus separated him from them after his accession. He was considered the offspring of the gods, and upon death, he would return to them after achieving apotheosis, earning the title of divus in due course.

Upon closer inspection, it becomes clear that the partitions were not watertight, and powerful equalitarian forces continued to circulate, constantly stirring up and renewing the elements of a society with deeply entrenched divisions. We need, therefore, feel no surprise that, at the time a new idea of merit and advancement was applied to imperial dignity, it would naturally circulate throughout the entire empire to revitalize and rejuvenate.

Rome under the Antonines was a meeting place where Romans from Rome encountered inferior peoples against whom their laws seemed to have erected solid ethnic barriers. Alternatively, Rome was a melting pot in which, despite its laws, the peoples were continually being subjected to new processes of assimilation. They had always treated their slaves with consideration, as Cato had treated his plough oxen. However far back we go in history, we find the Romans spurred their slaves into effort by offering them pay and bonuses, which accumulated to form a nest egg that, as a rule, served ultimately to buy their freedom. Slavery in Rome was not eternal and, during its duration, was not unbearable. However, it was never as easy to escape as it was under the Antonine dynasty.

Appian of Alexandria, writing under Antoninus Pius, notes that even in external appearances, a slave was not distinguishable from his master. However, unless his master wore the toga praetexta of a magistrate, the two would dress alike. Appian supplements this by recording a remarkable event: after a slave regained his freedom, he lived on equal terms with a Roman citizen. He established a lower category of citizens, known as Latini Iuniani, who were granted partial naturalization of the Ius Latii. However, this privilege excluded the holder from inheriting or benefiting from a will. D Thirty-five years before, and thirty years later, only one of the forty-five patrician families remained. Many of these individuals were conscientious and honest in their work, serving as faithful and devoted agents. The fact that the machinery of empire functioned so smoothly during this period was likely due more to the diligence and expertise of its mechanics than to the vigilance of its engineers.

Betrothals were a common practice in ancient Rome, and Pliny the Younger considered them one of the many trivial matters that occupied the time of his contemporaries. Martial tells an illuminating little anecdote. Among the statues of wild beasts in the Portico of a Hundred Pillars stood a bronze bear. One day, it caught the attention of the idle onlookers. As they watched, Hylas, engaged in play, challenged the bear's yawning mouth by plunging his youthful hand into its throat. Augustus would often host his family for a game of par imparr at the palace. He allocated each of them 250 denarii to enjoy the game without any worries or regrets. As played by the imperial family, the game consisted of betting on the odd or even number of objects (pebbles, nuts, or knuckle-bones) hidden in the other player's hand. Not infrequently, inns (cauponae) and taverns {popinae and thermopolia), whose front counters served cooling draughts or hot wine, concealed gambling dens in their back premises. Bets could be exchanged there year-round, not just during the Saturnalia, where dice and knuckle-bones were often rattled.

We seem to see a new feeling come to birth, all in honour of the 'Brothers' of Lanuvium: a sense of brotherhood that unites them in life and will reunite them in death. In anticipation of their death, they meet together to jointly subscribe for the cost of their respective obsequies, with the hope of winning salvation in another world. This same feeling of brotherhood, even stronger because nourished by a higher ideal and enlightened by the truths of the Gospel, drew together the Christians of Rome at the end of their day's work, in those cenae to which their communities had given the Greek name 'Agape'.

All in all, then, although they often fail us, our literary documents must be the starting point for any serious study of daily life in ancient Rome.
